steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content:
The claim focuses on a specific satirical critique: sophists in the play are portrayed as teaching students to be persuasive regardless of moral good.

The strongest clue is the explicit mention of a method that includes instruction in arguments that maintain what is unjust—this directly aligns with the claim.

steps.title: Match the meaning
steps.content:
Choice D includes a quotation that explicitly states the sophist should teach both a moral and an unjust style, with an emphasis on the unjust one to "overturn the better." This captures the satire by showing the sophist’s disregard for moral good through direct instruction in unethical persuasion techniques.

The other three either discuss hiring/soliciting a sophist but omit the moral critique (B), reference intellectual methods unrelated to persuasion (A), or suggest Socrates’ pedagogy without mentioning unethical argumentation (C).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
